steps.dialogue: Let's see how your math skills have grown by seeing how you can build fractions from unit parts.
[event:68fbc2ad0d9dae96]Look at these three bars. Which one shows [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction]? Remember, that's three unit fractions combined together.

prompt.text: Select the bar that shows [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction].

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Not quite. Let's count the shaded parts in each bar. We need exactly three shaded parts.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Try to check carefully. Watch as we [event:696aa698b12cb924]count up to the fraction. See which bar gets to [fraction numerator=3, denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction].

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let me count for you. First bar: [event:697d3d1afef00934]one piece shaded. That's [fraction numerator=1, denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction]. Middle bar: [event:697d3d352840a91a]one, two pieces shaded. That's [fraction numerator=2, denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction]. Last bar: [event:697d3d4ee6e8359d]one, two, three parts shaded. That's [fraction numerator=3, denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Nice work. You recognized [fraction numerator=3, denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction], three parts [event:697d3bbaa794394a]created from unit fractions.

steps.dialogue: [event:696aaeee6db57b23]Look at this empty bar with 6 parts. Please shade the parts to make [fraction numerator=4, denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction]. Start with [fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction], then shade more to make [fraction numerator=4, denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction].

prompt.text: Click parts to shade [fraction numerator=4, denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction].

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Here's a hint: Try counting as you shade, and remember we need four parts.
[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Remember,[event: step_7_md_1] we need exactly four parts shaded.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Watch as we count up[event: step_7_hv_1]: one part[event: step_7_hv_2], two parts[event: step_7_hv_3], three parts[event: step_7_hv_4], four parts[event:691b4853aee2c980]. That makes four sixths.

on_correct.dialogue: Nice. You make [fraction numerator=4, denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] by combining four unit fractions: [event:697d4236a7439758][fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction] + [fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction] + [fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction] + [fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction]. That's exactly how building fractions works.

steps.dialogue: [event:696ab5b75d23e540]Next, let's add unit fractions to make one whole. Remember what you learned about [fraction numerator=3, denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction].

prompt.text: Shade all parts to make 1 whole.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Remember, to make one whole, shade every part.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Not quite. To make one whole, you need to shade all three parts [event: step_10_md_1]. [fraction numerator=3, denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] means three parts out of three total.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let me show you. I add them up: [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction][event: step_10_hv_1]  + [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction][event: step_10_hv_2] + [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction][event:696ab7deeaab2bb6] = [fraction numerator=3, denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction]. All three parts shaded makes [fraction numerator=3, denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction], and [fraction numerator=3, denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] equals exactly 1 whole.

on_correct.dialogue: That's it. You built [event:697d447b4603d50d][fraction numerator=3, denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction], which equals 1. When we fill all the parts, we get 1.

steps.dialogue: You've got it! You can make any fraction by adding unit fractions together.[d:0.5] When the top and bottom numbers match, that equals 1 whole. Now let's strengthen this skill with different challenges.
